mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 540i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B58 3.0-litre engine typically costs £4,500–£8,000 supply only. Used engines are available from £3,500–£5,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £10,000.

Yes — the B58 is widely considered one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. It's a significant step forward from the N55, with improved cooling, durability, and reduced friction. However, it has known weak points: oil filter housing leaks, PCV diaphragm issues, and water pump failures. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 540i?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are oil filter housing leaks (plastic housing cracks, causing oil and coolant to mix), PCV valve diaphragm failure (causing rough idle and smoke), water pump and thermostat failures, and VANOS solenoid wear. The B58 is generally robust but these are known, manageable weak points.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 540i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and which component has failed. Against a well-kept £25,000 540i, a £6,000 recon is 24% — worth it. Against a tired £10,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 540i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 540i carries the engine code B58B30 (with various suffixes like B58B30A, B58B30C, or B58B30M1 depending on market and year). It's a 3.0-litre straight-six turbo petrol engine. The B58 is part of BMW's modular engine family.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the oil filter housing leak, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — oil filter housing replacement is a well-understood repair costing £500–£1,200. The housing is made of plastic and is prone to cracking. If the housing fails, oil and coolant can mix, leading to catastrophic engine damage. An aftermarket metal housing upgrade is available for a permanent fix. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have it inspected immediately.

problems.title: Oil Filter Housing Leaks
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning, visible oil residue around the oil filter housing area, coolant loss without visible external leaks (indicating internal seal failure causing oil and coolant to mix).
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,200 (oil filter housing replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if oil and coolant mix, catastrophic engine failure can occur

problems.recommendation: The B58's oil filter housing is made of brittle plastic and is prone to cracking and leaking. If the housing fails internally, oil and coolant can mix — a potentially catastrophic failure. Many owners of older B58s have experienced a leaky oil filter housing. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the oil filter housing inspected promptly. An aftermarket metal housing upgrade is available for a permanent fix.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

problems.title: PCV Valve Diaphragm Failure (Gen 1 B58)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, whistling noise from the engine, white or blue smoke from exhaust on startup, oil in the intake/chargepipe, misfires, shudder during operation, reduced power (limp mode).
problems.repairCost: £200–£500 (PCV diaphragm repair kit)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The PCV diaphragm on first-generation B58 engines can tear, causing high crankcase vacuum and smoking. The stock diaphragm is fragile and can crack or tear over time. The good news is this is easily fixed with an affordable repair kit that doesn't require replacing the entire valve cover. If you notice rough idle, whistling, or smoke on startup, have the PCV system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full PCV failure guide

problems.title: Water Pump & Cooling System Failures
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature gauge fluctuations, engine warning light, sweet smell from the engine bay, engine fan running constantly.
problems.repairCost: £400–£900 (water pump replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The B58's electric water pump is prone to failure around 70k–100k miles. The thermostat is often replaced with the pump due to shared failure rates. Cooling system complexity with more hoses, expansion tanks, and plastic components means more potential leak points over time. If you're experiencing coolant loss or overheating, have the cooling system inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 540i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with evidence of regular oil changes using BMW LL-01 approved oil — frequent oil services every 5k miles are ideal
* Oil filter housing inspected or replaced with upgraded metal unit
* Cooling system service records (water pump, thermostat replaced)
* No rough idle, whistling, or smoke on startup
avoidIf: * Oil leaks with no documented oil filter housing inspection or replacement
* Coolant loss with no cooling system service records
* Rough idle or whistling from the engine (PCV diaphragm warning)
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 540i's B58 is one of BMW's finest modern engines — a genuinely robust, powerful, and tunable straight-six that represents a significant step forward from the N55. It's not bulletproof — oil filter housing leaks, PCV diaphragm issues, and water pump failures are the price of entry — but these are well-understood, manageable fixes. The closed-deck block design, forged internals, and better cooling make it far more robust than many have expected. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained 540i is a £25,000–£40,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £7,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare 540i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 540i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rough idle, whistling, or smoke (PCV diaphragm warning)
* Service History — including the B58 era (2017–2023) and confirmed oil-change intervals using BMW LL-01 approved oil — ideally every 5k miles
* Oil Leak Check — Any history of oil leaks? (oil filter housing is a known weak point)
* Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or water pump/thermostat replacement?
* Smoke Check — White or blue smoke on startup can indicate PCV or turbo issues
* VANOS Operation — Any hesitation, rough idle, or loss of power? (VANOS solenoid warning)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved VANOS, cooling system, or PCV issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
